attributes.stem: A 32-year-old woman is undergoing an elective caesarean section under spinal anaesthesia. Ten minutes after administration of intrathecal bupivacaine, she complains of difficulty breathing and numbness in her arms. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears anxious. Her pulse rate is 120/min, blood pressure 85/50 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 94% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. She has loss of power in her arms and reports that her voice feels weak.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most important management step?
attributes.explanation:
This patient is developing a high spinal block, characterised by ascending motor weakness, respiratory compromise, and hypotension. The involvement of the arms (typically T1-C8) and complaint of voice weakness (suggesting impending phrenic nerve paralysis) indicate that the block is ascending to a dangerously high level.

Rapid sequence induction and intubation is the most appropriate immediate management as the patient is at risk of complete respiratory failure and loss of consciousness. Early airway intervention is crucial before the situation deteriorates further.

Lateral tilt and fluid bolus alone would be insufficient. While these measures help manage hypotension from spinal anaesthesia, they do not address the immediate threat to the airway and ventilation in a high spinal.

Noradrenaline infusion would help manage the hypotension but does not address the primary concern of impending respiratory failure. Vasopressor support will likely be needed but only after securing the airway.

Supplemental oxygen alone is inadequate as the patient is developing respiratory muscle paralysis. While oxygen should be administered, it will not prevent progression to respiratory arrest.

Emergency caesarean delivery should not delay airway management. The baby can be delivered once the mother's airway is secured and she is haemodynamically stable.

After securing the airway, management includes vasopressor support, mechanical ventilation, and completion of the surgery. The block will typically wear off in 2-4 hours, at which point extubation can be considered if the patient meets appropriate criteria.
learningPoints: High or total spinal block is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate airway management and cardiovascular support.
Loss of consciousness and respiratory arrest are late signs of high spinal block, occurring after initial sympathetic blockade.
Early recognition of ascending motor block and respiratory difficulty allows prevention of cardiac arrest through prompt intubation.
references: Stewart, A., Coathup, R. (2022). Recognition and Management of High Spinal Anaesthesia. In: Fernando, R., Sultan, P., Phillips, S. (eds) Quick Hits in Obstetric Anesthesia. Springer, Cham. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-72487-0_65
